voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: South America — tropical and subtropical rainforest canopy
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: It relies mainly on smell and touch to move through the dark canopy.
Long whiskers feel branches and gaps in the canopy at night.
Its nose detects food and the scent marks of other porcupines on branches.
Its hearing picks up sounds of predators and unfamiliar movement below.
Where does it live — and why?: It spends over 85 percent of its time in the rainforest canopy.
It sleeps in high branches, tree forks, or hollow trunks during the day.
It eats leaves, bark, roots, and fruit found in the trees.
It rarely touches the ground, moving between trees by reaching from branch to branch.
How does it send messages?: It raises its quills perpendicular to its body when threatened.
It turns sideways to look larger in front of an approaching predator.
It moans, grunts, hisses, and chatters to communicate with other porcupines.
Scent from flank glands marks the branches and trees it uses regularly.
What does it do when something changes?: During the day it stays still and hidden in the tree canopy.
It wakes at dusk and moves to a different tree each night.
When threatened, it stands on its hind legs and growls before using its quills.
It curls its body to protect its soft underside from predators.
How does it learn?: The single young stays near its mother and learns which trees to visit.
It is born with open eyes and climbs trees within a few days.
It gradually learns the canopy routes the mother uses before foraging independently.
How does it have babies?: After about 203 days, the female gives birth to one large young.
The newborn has soft, flexible spines that harden within hours after birth.
It is born with its eyes open and can grip branches almost immediately.
The young porcupine nurses from the mother while also beginning to try solid food.
What are its limits?: It cannot jump — it must physically reach each new tree by branch.
Loss of canopy cover cuts off its travel routes and food sources.
Daylight activity is almost entirely absent — exposure during the day increases predation risk.
Jaguars hunt it on the rare occasions it touches the ground.
What does this teach us?: Never needing the ground, it is shaped entirely by the canopy above.
Its quills, scent glands, and nocturnal timing are three different tools for staying safe.
It drops seeds and gnaws bark, shaping the forest as it feeds.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Spends over 85% of its time in the canopy and rarely touches the ground.
Cannot jump — every between-tree move requires a physical branch connection.
Raises quills and turns sideways as a staged threat display before contact defense.
Produces a single well-developed young with soft spines that harden within hours.
still_debated: How scent gland marking interacts with tree-use overlap between individuals.
Whether the young truly becomes independent before reaching adult body size.
How much daytime activity is possible under specific low-pressure conditions.
common_failure_modes: Describing it as similar to North American porcupines — different genus, different ecology.
Assuming it 'shoots' its quills — it does not.
Underestimating canopy-connectivity requirements when discussing habitat.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not say porcupines throw or shoot quills.
Do not treat fragmented forest as survivable habitat; it cannot cross gaps.
Do not confuse with Old World porcupines (Hystrix) — different family.
